improvement_actions.action: Improvement action generation failed — review transcript manually.

transcript.content:
This is a structured assessment — about 15 minutes, different question types, no right or wrong answers. It adapts as it learns, so the questions will shift based on what you share. Answer honestly, not ideally — that's where the real signal is.

When something feels wrong in your life — a relationship, a situation, a decision you made — what's the first thing that happens inside you?

response_parts.question_text: When something feels wrong in your life — a relationship, a situation, a decision you made — what's the first thing that happens inside you?
response_parts.context_note: Opening question targeting dominant center identification

strategy.what_was_learned: User is ready to begin. No data yet.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Opening question probes first internal response to distress — distinguishes gut reaction (body center: anger/wrongness), emotional flood (heart center: shame/loss), or mental spiral (head center: worry/analysis). Most revealing question for dominant center identification.

transcript.content:
*short pause*

Uh... honestly? I think I just — I get this like, restless energy? Like I suddenly need to be doing something else, somewhere else. I'll open like six browser tabs or text someone about doing something that weekend. 

*laughs quietly*

It's very efficient, actually. Highly recommend it as a coping mechanism. Zero stars on the long-term results though, I'll give you that much.
The joke landed before the answer did — that's worth noticing. When you actually do stay with something uncomfortable — don't move, don't distract, just sit there — what happens?

lexicon_signals.context: describing internal response to something feeling wrong

oyn_dimensions.who: Self-aware, deflects with humor, moves fast when uncomfortable
oyn_dimensions.why: Avoidance of sitting with discomfort — classic 7 flight response

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Restless escape — movement away from discomfort
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Humor as partial self-awareness — knows it's a pattern
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Avoidance of pain/stillness — zero long-term results noted

internal_block.thinking_display: The joke landed before the answer did.

response_parts.question_text: When you actually do stay with something uncomfortable — don't move, don't distract, just sit there — what happens?

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong 7 signal: movement away from discomfort, planning as escape, humor as deflection. Head center highly probable. Need to probe what happens when escape isn't available — tests avoidance depth.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Test what's underneath the movement. If 7, sitting still is intolerable. If 6, anxiety surfaces. If 3, they reframe it as productive. Probe body center indirectly.

transcript.content:
*laughs a little, shifts*

Honestly? I don't love finding out. Like, I'll get maybe thirty seconds in and suddenly remember I was gonna look up that new synthesizer plugin, or I'll convince myself that discomfort is just... inefficiency, you know? Bad signal, move on. 

*pause*

The few times I actually haven't bailed — and I mean actually haven't — it gets kind of loud in there. Not like dramatic loud, just... a lot of tabs open, if that makes sense. Stuff I didn't know was still running.

But I mean, that's what walks are for, right? Get it out of the system. 

*quick smile*

When you're moving toward something — making plans, chasing an idea — which of these fits better?

A) You're drawn to what's possible, what's next, what you haven't experienced yet.
B) You're trying to get somewhere that feels settled and low-friction.

oyn_dimensions.who: deflects discomfort with humor and reframing; self-aware about the pattern
oyn_dimensions.what: restless energy, tab-opening, distraction as automatic response

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: avoidance through movement, humor, reframing — turns discomfort into noise to outrun
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: acknowledged the few times they stayed — 'a lot of tabs open, stuff still running' — shows capacity for presence
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: quick mind, reframing ability, self-aware humor
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: treats inner discomfort as inefficiency — dismisses it before it can speak

internal_block.thinking_display: The walk comment wasn't a coping tip — it was a closing argument.

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong 7 signals: avoidance via mental motion, reframing discomfort as inefficiency, self-deprecating humor that closes the loop before the feeling lands. 9 possible — the 'restless energy' could be 9's dissociation but the framing reads more 7 (efficiency language, tab metaphor is active/mental, not passive/drifting). Need to probe body/gut center next and test whether this is planning-oriented future-focus (7) or conflict-averse disengagement (9). Also want to check if there's a fear of missing out or a fear of being trapped — key 7 differentiator.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Shift to forced_choice to break the open format streak. Test the 7/9 differentiation axis: 7s move toward MORE (options, experiences, future plans) while 9s move toward LESS (friction, stimulation, demand). A question about what they're actually running toward vs away from will help separate these. Want to probe gut center — what actually matters to them in terms of commitment or follow-through.

transcript.content:
Oh, A, definitely A. Like, without question. 

The second one sounds like — I don't know, it sounds like retirement? No offense to retirement, but I'm twenty-five, I'm not trying to optimize for *low-friction*. That phrase alone kind of stresses me out, honestly.

The thing that gets me out of bed is usually some idea I had at 1am that I convinced myself was genius, or a new project, or — okay, last month I literally bought a beginner's ceramics kit because I watched one YouTube video and thought, yeah, that's the thing. That's my thing now.

*[laughs]*

So yeah. What's possible, what's next. That's the engine.
When a project or idea starts to feel like work — like it's lost that early spark — how often do you actually push through versus pivot to something new?

Sometimes

Always

Need to probe heart center and differentiate from 3 (achievement/image vs experience/stimulation). Also need to surface what's underneath the movement — the fear.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Push-through vs pivot probes commitment tolerance and relationship to limitation — key 7 differentiator. Frequency format rotates from forced_choice.

transcript.content:
**Sometimes** — but honestly, "sometimes" is doing a lot of heavy lifting there.

Like, I *intend* to push through every time, I've got the whole speech ready — "real artists finish things," blah blah — but then something new comes along that's genuinely exciting, and it's not *not* working on things, it's just... strategic reallocation of creative energy? *laughs* No, okay, I hear myself. Sometimes I bail. The design contract I'm on right now is a good example of me *not* bailing, which — growth, right?
When you think about the people you're closest to — do you tend to go deep with a few, or keep a wider orbit of people you genuinely like but don't fully let in?

transcript.content:
**Wider orbit of many.**

Honestly? I just — I like people, like genuinely, across the board. My friend group is huge and we have *such* a good time, which to me that's the real thing, you know? I don't think "going deep" is the only valid currency in a friendship. *[short pause]* Besides, I move around a lot, always got something going on — it's easier to just... keep the energy up with everyone than to like, slow everything down for one person.

When someone you care about is going through something hard — grief, a real crisis — do you lean in and sit with them in it, or do you find yourself wanting to help them feel better and move through it faster?

Heart center underprobed — need to check whether image/success (3) is also running.

transcript.content:
**Help them feel better and move through it faster.**

I mean... yeah, that's honestly me. I'm like, okay, what do we *do* about this, let's get you out of the house, have you eaten, here's a meme that's weirdly relevant — *laughs* — I'm very much a "let's get moving" type when people are hurting. Sitting in it feels like... I don't know, it feels like it makes things worse? Like you're just marinating in the bad stuff. Though I'll admit sometimes I can tell the person kind of needed me to just *stay there* and I kind of... didn't.

That automatic version — the "let's get moving" — runs before you choose it. When you're truly stuck and can't pivot your way out — no new project, no exit ramp — what shows up?

A) Anxiety — my mind goes to what could go wrong.
B) Frustration — I just need things to start moving again.

transcript.content:
**Frustration — I just need things to start moving again.**

...I mean, that's the cleaner answer. If I'm being weirdly honest, there's probably some anxiety underneath it, but it kind of *disguises* itself as frustration, you know? Like, "this is boring and stuck" feels way more manageable to say out loud than whatever the other thing is. *laughs* But yeah — frustration. Definitely frustration. Moving on.
When you finish something you're proud of — a project, a design, anything — what matters more to you: how it lands with other people, or the rush of having made it?

transcript.content:
*laughs a little too quickly* 

Okay, honestly? The rush. Definitely the rush. Like, there's this window right after you finish something where it just *exists* and it's yours and nobody's had a chance to have an opinion about it yet, and that's kind of the best it's ever gonna feel, you know?

...Although. I mean, I'd be lying if I said I didn't check the Instagram metrics like four times in a row after posting something. So maybe it's more complicated than that. But the external stuff always ends up being kind of a letdown anyway — people like it, cool, people don't, whatever, the feeling fades either way. The making part is the part that actually *does* something.

*pauses, then adds with a grin* 

Which probably explains why I have seventeen unfinished projects on my desktop, so. There's a flaw in the system somewhere.

When you imagine a version of yourself who's genuinely successful — whatever that means to you — is that person known for something, or are they just living exactly how they want?

A) Known for something specific
B) Living exactly how they want — recognition isn't really the point

transcript.content:
**Living exactly how they want — recognition isn't really the point.**

I mean... okay, *technically* both would be sick, but if I had to pick — yeah, it's more about the vibe than the fame, you know? Like, I don't need a Wikipedia page. I just want to wake up and feel like every day has something good in it, something interesting happening. 

*[short pause]*

...Although I guess if people happened to think my work was cool, I wouldn't exactly be upset about that.

When you have a completely open day — no plans, no obligations, nothing on the calendar — do you feel free, or do you feel a quiet pressure to fill it with something?

A) Free — open space is genuinely good
B) Pressure — I'll fill it before the day starts

transcript.content:
**Pressure — I'll fill it before the day starts**

Honestly, a totally blank day sounds great in theory — like, *yes, finally* — but I've definitely already texted three people and opened a new tab before I've had coffee. I don't know, free time feels kind of... loud? Like it's just sitting there waiting for you to do something interesting with it. So yeah, I fill it. Usually with something fun though, so it's not like *stressful* pressure, it's more like... productive enthusiasm. *laughs* That's what I'm calling it.

When you're working on something and it's going well — really clicking — is the best part the feeling of doing it, or that you're getting somewhere, building toward something that will matter?

A) The feeling of doing it — the process itself
B) Getting somewhere — building toward something that matters

transcript.content:
**The feeling of doing it — the process itself.**

Like, the "building toward something" part sounds great in theory, but honestly the second something starts feeling like it's *only* about the destination I kind of... lose the thread? The best moments are when you're just in it — the flow, the vibe, everything clicking. That's the whole thing for me. The finished product is almost like a byproduct, which — okay, yeah, I hear how that sounds for someone with deadlines. *laughs* But the process is where it's alive, you know?

When you're genuinely stretched thin — real stress, not just busy — do you scatter outward into more things, or lock down and push harder on the one thing in front of you?

A) Scatter — more projects, more directions, more people
B) Lock down — harder focus on the one thing

transcript.content:
**More scattered — more things, more directions.**

Ha, yeah, no contest there. When things get heavy I basically become a human browser with forty tabs open — like, *oh you need to finish this design project? Cool, have you considered learning to play the kalimba instead?* It's not great, I know, but honestly sometimes stepping away sparks something. That's what I tell myself anyway.

When you're uncertain about a decision — like you genuinely don't know which way to go — do you trust your own read on it, or do you find yourself looking for what other people think before you commit?

transcript.content:
**I trust my own read — I'll figure it out.**

Mostly, yeah. Like, I'm not gonna sit around polling people before I make a move — that sounds exhausting. I'll just... commit to something and course-correct if it goes sideways. *pauses* I mean, I'll casually mention the thing to friends and see how they react, but that's just, like, conversation, not research. Totally different.

When you're doing something and it stops being fun — the spark's gone, it's become obligation — do you feel more like you're losing something, or more like people might start seeing you as someone who doesn't follow through?

A) Losing something — the experience itself is what mattered
B) People seeing me as someone who doesn't follow through

when asked about what shows up when stuck

defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Avoidance disguised as enthusiasm — pain reframed as 'bad signal, move on'

People seeing me as someone who doesn't follow through

transcript.content:
**Losing something — the experience itself is what mattered.**

Like, the second something starts feeling like a chore, the whole vibe of it is already dead, you know? It's not like I'm sitting there worried about my reputation or whatever — I'm more just... grieving the version of it that used to be exciting. Which sounds dramatic when I say it out loud. *laughs* But yeah, it's more about the loss of the thing than what anyone thinks about me.

When you're honest with yourself about why you start most things — is it more because you genuinely want the experience, or because part of you believes this might be the thing that finally works out?

A) The experience — I want to live it
B) The payoff — part of me thinks this one might stick

words: the process is where it's alive
lexicon_signals.context: across multiple responses about motivation and work
secondary signals around achievement and image

oyn_dimensions.who: 25-year-old designer, wide social orbit, moves frequently
oyn_dimensions.what: collects experiences and projects, avoids completion
oyn_dimensions.why: fear of being trapped in pain/boredom; joy as survival strategy

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Automatic pivot away from discomfort — browser tabs, new projects, movement
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: The design contract — staying when it got hard. Named it as growth.
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Genuine enthusiasm, idea generation, bringing energy and lightness to others
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Avoidance disguised as optimism — 'productive enthusiasm', 'strategic reallocation'

internal_block.thinking_display: The grief is real — but it's for the feeling, not the finished thing.

response_parts.question_text: When you're honest with yourself about why you start most things — is it more because you genuinely want the experience, or because part of you believes this might be the thing that finally works out?
answer_options: The experience — I want to live it
The payoff — part of me thinks this one might stick

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong 7 signal confirmed: process over destination, experience over recognition, scatter under stress, avoidance of sustained discomfort. 3 ruled out — image/recognition not primary. Body center still unprobed. Disconfirmatory question needed before close.
strategy.next_question_rationale: 7v3 disconfirmatory — if leading type is 7, they should answer 'the experience.' If 3, they'd answer around payoff/validation. Also serves as the final differentiation gate. After this, ready to close.
